did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (specifically a U-Net architecture with a ResNet50 encoder) to perform lesion segmentation on a large-scale dermatopathology dataset. The study trained and evaluated this AI model to segment six types of skin lesions (epidermal cysts, seborrheic keratosis, Bowen disease/squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, and malignant melanoma) and normal skin, reporting Dice scores ranging from 80% to 91%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' and describes the construction of a large-scale dermatopathology dataset (34,376 images) and the development/evaluation of a deep learning segmentation model (U-Net with ResNet50 encoder). It presents original experimental results, including dataset quality metrics (syntactic/semantic accuracy) and model performance (Dice scores 80-91%), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Large-Scale Dermatopathology Dataset for Lesion Segmentation: Model Development and Analysis
brief_summary: This study constructed a large-scale, multi-institutional dermatopathology dataset comprising 34,376 whole-slide images from four Korean medical institutions to support AI-driven lesion segmentation. The dataset includes annotations for normal skin and six common skin lesions: epidermal cysts, seborrheic keratosis, Bowen disease/squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, and malignant melanoma. To ensure data quality, the researchers performed syntactic and semantic accuracy assessments, achieving scores of 0.99 and 0.95, respectively. A U-Net segmentation model with a ResNet50 encoder was trained and evaluated on this dataset to assess its validity for deep learning applications. The model demonstrated strong performance with Dice scores ranging from 80% to 91% across different lesion types at the patch and slide levels. The results indicate that the constructed dataset is a high-quality resource for advancing AI-based dermatopathology diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Group 1; 5,036 cases (14.65% of total); patch-level Dice score 90.1%, slide-level 81.9%.
Group 2; 5,133 cases (14.93% of total); patch-level Dice score 89.9%, slide-level 90.2%.
Group 3; 5,031 cases (14.64% of total); patch-level Dice score 82.0%, slide-level 81.3%.
Group 4; 5,419 cases (15.76% of total); patch-level Dice score 88.9%, slide-level 85.3%.
Group 5; 5,036 cases (14.65% of total); patch-level Dice score 84.3%, slide-level 90.8%.
Group 6; 3,000 cases (8.73% of total); patch-level Dice score 87.4%, slide-level 81.7%.

paper_terms: normal skin tissue - non-neoplastic skin samples with nonspecific histologic findings
lesion_types_studied.details: Group 7; 5,721 cases (16.64% of total); used as a class for segmentation alongside lesions.

all_task_types.target: Lesion area annotations (segmentation masks) for normal skin and six types of skin lesions (epidermal cysts, seborrheic keratosis, Bowen disease/squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, and malignant melanoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim to build a dataset for 'lesion segmentation' and trained a U-Net model to segment lesions. Performance was evaluated using Dice scores at patch and slide levels for seven classes (normal skin + 6 lesion types). While the dataset includes classification labels, the AI model's primary task described is segmentation, not classification.

datasets_used.name: Multi-institutional Dermatopathology Dataset (Chong et al., 2025)
datasets_used.description: A large-scale dataset of 34,376 whole-slide images (WSIs) collected from four Korean medical institutions: Seoul National University Hospital, Catholic Medical Center, National Cancer Center, and Samsung Medical Center. The dataset includes histopathology slides of normal skin and six types of skin lesions, annotated with lesion area masks and clinical information. It was archived and shared via the National Information Society Agency (NIA) AI-Hub.

use.training: Used to train a U-Net segmentation model with a ResNet50 encoder. Data was split into training, validation, and test sets at an 8:1:1 ratio at the patient level.
use.validation: Used for validation of the segmentation model (10% of total data).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the segmentation model (10% of total data).
use.other: Archived and shared via the NIA AI-Hub platform for public research and development.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To verify the accuracy of the structure and format of the constructed labeled data file, checking for grammar issues, data structure problems, typos, or missing information.
To evaluate the correctness of the labeling region by verifying outlines by pathologists who did not participate in data establishment.
To verify the natural distribution of demographic data, including procedures, sex, age, and lesion location.
To evaluate the performance of the deep learning segmentation model at patch and slide levels.
Used as the objective function during model training to optimize segmentation performance.
To report the uncertainty of the mean Dice scores for model performance at patch and slide levels for each disease group.
To report the variability of Dice scores across the test set for each disease group.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study used descriptive statistics (mean, standard deviation, 95% confidence intervals) to report model performance metrics (Dice scores) without formal significance testing.

model.architecture: U-Net with ResNet50 encoder (pretrained on ImageNet)

Multi-institutional Dermatopathology Dat.notes: The model achieved the highest patch-level performance for Epidermal Cysts (90.1%) and the highest slide-level performance for Melanocytic Nevus (90.8%) and Seborrheic Keratosis (90.2%). Bowen Disease/Squamous Cell Carcinoma showed the lowest performance at both patch (82.0%) and slide (81.3%) levels. The dataset was archived and shared via the NIA AI-Hub.

training_details.data_normalization: Input image data was normalized beforehand

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: MPMR (Multi-Scale Feature and Probability Map for Melanoma)
preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of significantly poor images (e.g., poor fixation, dehydration, staining, sectioning, or scanning issues) and images with lesions too small to provide meaningful information
Data quality management including syntactic accuracy verification (checking for grammar issues, data structure problems, typos, or missing information)
Data quality management including semantic accuracy verification (outlines verified by pathologists who did not participate in data establishment)
Statistical diversity verification (checking distribution of procedures, sex, age, and lesion location)
Patch extraction from whole-slide images (WSIs) at a magnification of ×5
Conversion of WSI formats (.svs, .mrxs, .tiff) for annotation compatibility
conclusions: The constructed large-scale multi-institutional dermatopathology dataset (34,376 whole-slide images) is a high-quality resource for deep learning training, as evidenced by syntactic accuracy of 0.99, semantic accuracy of 0.95, and natural statistical diversity.

The U-Net segmentation model with a ResNet50 encoder demonstrated strong validity for lesion segmentation, achieving Dice scores ranging from 80% to 91% across different lesion types at both patch and slide levels.
The dataset provides a well-suited resource for advancing AI-driven dermatopathology diagnosis, addressing the insufficiency of large-scale skin pathology image datasets for AI learning.

Bowen’s disease/squamous cell carcinoma showed the lowest segmentation performance (82.0% patch-level, 81.3% slide-level), which aligns with the clinical challenge of visually distinguishing these lesions from normal or reactive squamous epithelium due to limited morphologic atypia.
The study highlights the intersection between real-world medical challenges and the limitations of deep learning models, particularly in distinguishing lesions with subtle histological differences.

This dataset is the most comprehensive dermatopathology image dataset available to date, covering normal skin and six common skin lesions, and is shared via the NIA AI-Hub platform to facilitate collaboration in AI development.
important_remarks: The dataset is exclusively sourced from four Korean medical institutions, limiting external generalizability to non-Korean or non-Asian populations.

Bowen disease/squamous cell carcinoma showed the lowest segmentation performance (82.0% patch-level, 81.3% slide-level), attributed to the clinical difficulty of distinguishing these lesions from normal or reactive squamous epithelium due to limited morphologic atypia.
The study did not perform formal hypothesis testing; results are reported using descriptive statistics (mean, standard deviation, 95% confidence intervals) without p-values.

The dataset is shared via the NIA AI-Hub platform, but the paper does not specify the specific licensing terms for commercial use beyond the article's Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License.
Data leakage risk is mitigated by splitting the data at the patient level (8:1:1 ratio) rather than at the image or slide level, ensuring no patient data appears in both training and test sets.
The study acknowledges that while the dataset is comprehensive, it focuses on common cutaneous neoplasms and may not represent the full spectrum of rare dermatopathological conditions.
One author (Young-Gon Kim) has a potential conflict of interest as a corporate officer of L’imagin Inc., though he declared no influence on the work.
